speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six. We're interviewing-- we're interviewing-- Doctor William Perl. And you were telling me about the ship being ordered to go to the nearest British port, Haifa. Can you finish the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was the people were on the boat and the British-- small crew came on the boat from a British warship, and they thought that we'll be in trouble now. And when the-- when the commander, the young lieutenant, who gives the order to proceed to the next British port and gave the Captain the order in writing. Suddenly, the British start to lead a song sung that which was familiar to them. It was-- It was very familiar to them. It was 'God Save the King.' Our people sang 'God Save the King' of this foolish British Navy, which sent us home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you about two other ships. I wanted to ask you about the

speaker: interviewee
text: The yes. It was our most traveled of all ships. There were people from Vienna and from Budapest some, but mainly Vienna and mainly Orthodox people from the [INAUDIBLE] which we wanted to take along for a long time, because they were traveling with [INAUDIBLE]. But we took them on the And the people arrived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by boat and they came by boat, I think. It could be they came by train because by then, the permission to bring them by train if you had a boat in the port. They went in sealed trains. I have a photo of this. I don't remember any more of how they came, but while the vessel in tow, on troops, there came reports of a serious disease. And they went to Cosenza to be boarding the ships there, and there it was assembled. First it was told that they have-- had some-- I don't remember the name of the disease now. Very serious disease, very infectious. And then it was that-- the disease was scarlet fever, which was a very dangerous disease at that time, too. And they were interned in a basement of a modern immigration hall. It was a modern building. It was the same where I had arrived and saw all the paper, all my life was into the air. And there they were interned for, I believe, two months, until the last one has-- has-- has had scarlet fever. And then we board the ship, the to bring them from Cosenza to Israel. And first of all, I had gone to Rhodes to get passengers in Rhodes in order to pay for the because the was there and was not used. And Rhodes, I saved all the young people from Rhodes because those who did not go on the boat, then illegally were all deported. When the German got their orders from the Italians, or they took over. They shipped the whole [INAUDIBLE] to Auschwitz, every single one of them. But we took a number of people, maybe 100 or 150 young people, from Rhodes who pay. We had the ship, the monies to take again. And to use the Was the trouble now, they loaded the people from Rhodes, and they was happy they left. And they was on-- on the bus to the airport to fly back to Athens to continue to work for other ship from Vienna as they came on. Always the desire of more more, more, we need more ships. We need more people. And I had then the worst two hours of my life, on the-- still today, the worst two hours. On the bus-- the bus, it was-- I always thought there will be more trouble. There was never anything without more trouble, more trouble. But I saw now on the bus, the people left, they are on their way and they're going to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were taken back to motor-- motorcycle policemen, who told us that the had caught fire and it sunk. Now, it was a catastrophe. People dead. How many dead? The transports were finished by this when the [INAUDIBLE] was obvious. But fortunately it was not that bad. The ship had caught fire, ran-- ran it into a rock-- I've got photos of it-- and everything, everyone was saved. The luggage was lost. And now was the trouble what to do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the-- the governor of Rhodes was a-- a friend of Mussolini's and he was a very good man. I spoke to him, and he called Mussolini for permission to keep all these people there, who were shipwrecked and who had nowhere to go anywhere now, and Mussolini agreed to it. I don't know that Mussolini agreed to it. I know that they were told they can stay. And they were interned now for a month or two on a-- on a-- stadium in Rhodes. The government and the Jewish community fed them through that time and finally they-- they got more steamer with two little [INAUDIBLE] hanging to it brought them to Israel. But I think this trip took about four or five months before they arrived. It was our most difficult trip. I had a letter in my hands a couple of days ago, which I think is here, of one of my fraternity brothers, who was on the ship, who sends me this-- years apart now-- I saved his life. It was on this ship. It was a very difficult trip. Yeah, you know, this fire was not a coincidence, I think. I believed it was the ship was taken not from the [INAUDIBLE], from another organization. And the crew or the owner, I think, put the ship afire. This way it was insured. He got his money back twice because we had paid already. We paid as the people boarded the ship. 10 more people, 120 pounds. Ten more people, 120 pounds. I was standing on the bridge, and paying cash as they went on board. And after that-- after that, I-- I think that-- put the ship afire. One of the sailors told it to me, that it was planned. You know, the sailors on these ships, the [INAUDIBLE] ships-- they were not good sailors. It-- it was the scum of the-- of all the ports in the Mediterranean. There's murderers and rapists and-- and people who had been in prisons for all kinds of crimes. They were the sailors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that? Why did-- why were those people the crew members on these ships?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were ready to take anything. They couldn't get any jobs. They were-- they were glad to have this job.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to also ask you about the

speaker: interviewee
text: The I believe this is the last ship that I can speak today about. The was a very good ship but the trouble was the rendezvous. The rendezvous with the [INAUDIBLE] in the fleet, and they couldn't land. The British ships, they were-- they chased us everywhere, but they kept to the three-mile zone. They confiscated you only if you went within three miles. but a ship couldn't get closer than three miles. And one day the people on board every ship had been brought-- a member of the Ergon decided to land-- to-- to land anyhow, without port in Warsaw and British ordered ships. The we broke through, straight headed for Tel Aviv Beach, and beached itself right on the center of Tel Aviv Beach. And the crowd assembled there, of course. And everyone was arrested who was on the boat, but I have pictures. They were happy. They were behind barbed wire, but they were happy they were at home. After a few months, they were released. This was soon followed by another ship, the [INAUDIBLE], and the ship which had traveled with the British, too, and it broke through, too and beached itself on the-- on the beach farther-- farther south or north-- farther north. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your estimate of how many Jews you helped-- you helped-- to get-- to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: About 17,000 to 18,000 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were in our ships. But all together about 40,000. I have the statistics in my-- in my book. The British figured-- the British had registered 20,100 and some immigrants between the fall of 1939 and the end of the war. But first of all, we started it long before 1939. In the fall of 1939, with several thousand in already. And then they didn't get everybody. We don't know of everybody, because besides us, there were steamer parties of 10 and 12 who left the Yugoslav port or Bulgarian ports, that's the family [INAUDIBLE] and made it there because they knew about it, that you can do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I estimate that the figure of 20,100 or so of the British can easily be doubled because they started counting late and they have, by far, not all the people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I think I have to end now. If you feel like we need to stop let's stop it.
